subject: ap-csp
theme: Computing innovations create profound societal benefits but also introduce risks — including privacy threats, algorithmic bias, and intellectual property challenges — that require ongoing ethical evaluation.

core_concepts.title: Intended vs. Unintended Effects
core_concepts.detail: Computing innovations have intended effects (their designed purpose) and unintended effects (unexpected positive or negative consequences). Evaluating both requires considering who is helped, who is harmed, and how data is used beyond its original purpose.

core_concepts.title: PII and Data Aggregation
core_concepts.detail: Personally identifiable information (PII) includes data that can identify, locate, or contact a specific individual. Even 'anonymized' data can become identifying when multiple data sources are combined — aggregating location, behavior, and demographics can re-identify individuals.

core_concepts.title: Computing Bias and Proxy Variables
core_concepts.detail: Computing bias occurs when algorithmic outputs systematically disadvantage or harm certain groups. It often originates in training data that underrepresents or misrepresents some populations. Bias can persist even without discriminatory intent through proxy variables (e.g., zip code as a proxy for race).

core_concepts.title: Cybersecurity Threats and Defenses
core_concepts.detail: Cybersecurity threats include phishing (deceptive emails impersonating trusted sources), malware (viruses, ransomware, keyloggers), and rogue access points (fake Wi-Fi networks that intercept traffic). Multi-factor authentication and encryption are key defenses.

core_concepts.title: Symmetric and Public Key Encryption
core_concepts.detail: Encryption converts plaintext into unreadable ciphertext using a key. Symmetric encryption uses the same key for both encryption and decryption. Public key encryption uses a public key to encrypt and a private key to decrypt — enabling secure communication without sharing a secret key in advance.

core_concepts.title: Copyright and Open Source Licensing
core_concepts.detail: Intellectual property (copyright) gives creators exclusive control over their original works. Creative Commons licenses grant specific permissions (e.g., attribution required, or non-commercial use only). Open source software provides code publicly but requires following the specific license terms.

core_concepts.title: Digital Divide and Crowdsourcing
core_concepts.detail: The digital divide is the gap in technology access across economic, geographic, and social lines — those without access miss educational and economic opportunities. Crowdsourcing harnesses public contributions for data collection, problem solving, and innovation, but participation biases can make crowdsourced data unrepresentative.

exam_tip: The AP exam tests your ability to identify intended vs. unintended consequences of computing, classify types of cybersecurity attacks, and evaluate privacy risks from data aggregation — know that combining multiple non-identifying data points can create identifying profiles.
